questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Dynamic programming applies when a problem has:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: DP caches solutions to overlapping subproblems that compose optimally.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Memoization is typically:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Compute as needed; store results to avoid recomputation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Tabulation is typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Bottom-up filling of a DP table
questions.explanation: Iterate dependencies in order so each state is ready when needed.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: 0/1 knapsack differs from unbounded knapsack in that:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Items may be taken unlimited times in 0/1
Each item may be chosen at most once
questions.explanation: 0/1 = at most one of each; unbounded = unlimited copies.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: A classic LIS (longest increasing subsequence) patience/DP insight is:? Which answer is best?
options: Always sort the array first destroying order
Maintain tails of increasing subsequences; binary search length extension
questions.explanation: O(n log n) via tails array; O(n²) DP compares prior smaller elements.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Fibonacci with naive recursion is slow because of:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: F(n) tree recomputes F(k) many times — DP/memo fixes it.

questions.stem: Space-optimizing a 1D DP that only depends on the previous index often uses:
options: A few rolling variables instead of a full array
questions.explanation: e.g., fib, climbing stairs: keep prev/prev2.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Coin change (fewest coins) with unlimited coins is a form of:? Choose the strongest option.
options: 0/1 knapsack with one coin type only
Greedy always optimal for all systems
questions.explanation: dp[x] = min over coins of dp[x-c]+1; greedy fails on some denominations.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: LCS (longest common subsequence) DP state typically indexes:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: dp[i][j] from first i and first j characters.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: When converting recursion + memo to tabulation, you must:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Order loops so dependencies are computed first
questions.explanation: Topological order of the state DAG.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A DP state design mistake is often:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Forgetting a dimension needed to enforce a constraint (e.g., last picked item)
questions.explanation: Missing state ⇒ incorrect transitions or illegal reuse.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Knapsack DP transition for 0/1 (item i, capacity w) chooses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: max(skip item, take item if w≥wt)
questions.explanation: Classic include/exclude. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why iterate capacity descending in 1D 0/1 knapsack? Which response is most accurate?
options: So each item is only used once (uses old values for higher caps)
questions.explanation: Ascending would reuse the same item like unbounded.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Edit distance DP allows operations:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: dp[i][j] min cost aligning prefixes.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Optimal substructure means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: An optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems
questions.explanation: Necessary DP property alongside overlapping work.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: If subproblems do not overlap, DP cache mainly:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Wastes memory — divide-and-conquer alone may suffice
questions.explanation: Overlapping is why memo pays off.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: House robber style DP chooses at each house:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Adjacent constraint encoded in transitions.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Matrix chain multiplication DP optimizes:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: dp[i][j] best split between i and j.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Boolean true/false DP for partition equal subset sum reduces to:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Classic subset-sum knapsack. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Top-down vs bottom-up: top-down may be better when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Only a sparse subset of states is reachable
questions.explanation: Memo computes only needed states; tabulation may fill everything.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Unbounded knapsack inner loop over capacity usually goes:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Ascending so the same item can be reused
questions.explanation: Opposite of 0/1 1D trick. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Bitmask DP is common when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: n is tiny (≤20–22) and state is a subset
questions.explanation: 2^n states for subsets; TSP Held-Karp style.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Digit DP interviews ask you to count numbers ≤ R with properties by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: DP on digit position + tight flag + other constraints
questions.explanation: Classic digit DP template. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Interval DP pattern fills by:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Smaller intervals before larger ones.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A rolling array for dp[i][j] depending on i-1 row means you can keep:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Two rows (or one carefully updated row)
questions.explanation: Space from O(n*m) to O(m) often.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: LIS O(n²) recurrence is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over j<i with a[j]<a[i] (or 1)
questions.explanation: Standard definition. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: Knapsack where order of items matters (combinations vs permutations) changes:
options: Loop nesting — coins outer vs amount outer affects counting combinations vs permutations
questions.explanation: Coin change number-of-ways pitfalls.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: DP on trees often roots the tree and defines state as:? Choose the strongest option.
options: f(u) from children — e.g., include/exclude u
questions.explanation: Rerooting / tree DP interview family.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: When greedy seems right but fails, a DP check is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Look for overlapping subproblems needing comparison of multiple choices
questions.explanation: Multiple viable subchoices ⇒ DP.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Catalan-number style DP counts:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Valid parentheses / BST structures / triangulations via convolution recurrence
questions.explanation: C_n = sum C_i C_{n-1-i}. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Probability DP (e.g., dice sums) stores:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Probability or ways to reach each sum/state
questions.explanation: Transitions distribute probability mass.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Metaprogramming bug: mutating a shared memo key object causes:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Keys must be immutable/value-based.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Shortest path in a DAG via DP/topo is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Relax edges in topological order once
questions.explanation: DAG SP is linear after topo. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Palindrome partition DP often combines:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Min cuts = classic interview DP.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: State compression for grid paths with obstacles still usually indexes:? Which answer is best?
options: (r,c) or bitmask of visited if small
questions.explanation: Choose minimal state that encodes constraints.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why reconstruct choice pointers (prev arrays) in DP? Which response is most accurate?
options: To recover the actual solution not just the optimal value
questions.explanation: Value DP + parent/choice recovery.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Floating-point DP for probabilities should watch for:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Precision error; sometimes use logs or exact fractions
questions.explanation: Interview + production numerical care.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: AtCoder-style 'pick or not with modulo' DP requires:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Transitions that add ways mod M without double-counting
questions.explanation: Careful include/exclude counting.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Comparing memo recursion depth vs tabulation:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Deep recursion may stack-overflow; tabulation/iterative memo avoids call depth
questions.explanation: Production risk on large n. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Optimization: monotone queue / convex hull tricks speed DP when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Transitions have structure (sliding window min, QI/CHT)
questions.explanation: Advanced DP speedups beyond O(n²).

questions.stem: Senior review: your 1D 0/1 knapsack loops capacity ascending. What's wrong?
options: Items can be reused — you accidentally solved unbounded
questions.explanation: Ascending + in-place ⇒ unbounded behavior.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Proving a DP correct in an interview: you should argue:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Induction on state order: base cases hold; transitions consider all legal choices
questions.explanation: Structure the proof around the DAG of states.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Alias bug: using global mutable dp across test cases without reset causes:? Which answer is best?
questions.explanation: Clear or reallocate per test. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: When DP state space is huge but answer is a count modulo prime, generating functions / combinatorics might:
options: Replace heavy DP with closed forms or NTT convolutions
questions.explanation: Senior alternative when patterns exist.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Open vs closed interval DP off-by-one errors usually come from:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Define clearly: dp[l][r] covers s[l..r] inclusive, etc.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Rerooting DP on trees lets you answer f(root)=u for all u in:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Typically O(n) after two DFS passes
questions.explanation: Contribute/exclude child contributions carefully.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Memory vs time: storing parent pointers for path recovery on huge DP may:? Which answer is best?
options: Blow memory — regenerate path by re-walking transitions instead
questions.explanation: Trade space for recompute. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: SOS DP (sum over subsets) is used when you need:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Fast zeta transforms over subset lattices
questions.explanation: Advanced bit DP technique. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: A DP that seems to need O(n) state but transitions scan O(n) each may still be improvable if:
options: You find incremental/sliding structure or convexity
questions.explanation: Look for optimization structure before accepting O(n²).

questions.stem: Interview red flag: 'DP' solution that recomputes without cache and claims O(n) because 'each call is O(1)' — response?
options: Ask for the recursion tree size / number of distinct states visited
questions.explanation: Complexity = states × transition work, counting multiplicity without memo.
